pi_items.source: Explicitly stated in the certification brief generated by setup_task.sh.
Derived from the context of the Airbus Helicopters High Altitude SAR Certification brief in setup_task.sh.
Stated in the prompt ('Time period: May') and enforced by verifier.py logic.
The prompt prohibits Sea Level Pressure (SLP) and demands Surface Pressure. NCEP's pres.mon.ltm.nc contains surface pressure.

Python analysis of pres.mon.ltm.nc shows surface pressure at May near 28N, 87E is ~69600 Pa (interpolated) or ~72500 Pa (nearest grid point 27.5N, 87.5E), which contradicts the expected range of 45000-65000. The dataset's 2.5-degree resolution smooths out the Himalayan topography, resulting in a much higher surface pressure than the true mountain peak.

Python analysis of pres.mon.ltm.nc shows surface pressure at May near 28N, 87E is ~69600-72500 Pa, exceeding the claimed max threshold of 65000 Pa. The claimed bounds clash with the coarse spatial resolution of the actual provided dataset.
Required path defined in the prompt and verified by export_result.sh and verifier.py.
Required filenames listed in the prompt and explicitly checked by export_result.sh.

privileged_info_summary: The task requires using real NCEP Reanalysis data (pres.mon.ltm.nc) downloaded from NOAA to find May surface pressure near the Himalayas. While the verifier expects a pressure between 45,000 and 65,000 Pa (consistent with real-world Everest altitudes), the provided dataset has a coarse 2.5-degree resolution. As a result, the 'surface' elevation is heavily smoothed, yielding an actual pressure of ~69,600 Pa (interpolated) or ~72,500 Pa (nearest grid point 27.5N, 87.5E) near 28°N, 87°E. Thus, extracting the correct value from the exact coordinate in the data provided will paradoxically fail the verifier's physics bounds. The required output files and paths are correctly defined.
